levels.title: Learn pico-pydantic
levels.description: Automatic argument validation with Pydantic models. Decorate any service method with @validate and let pico-pydantic coerce and check every call — no matter who the caller is.

levels.title: Why Validate at the Service Layer?
levels.description:
HTTP Validation Is Not Enough

Frameworks like FastAPI validate incoming HTTP requests beautifully. But your services are not only called from HTTP handlers. They can be invoked from:

  • CLI scripts
  • Background workers (Celery, cron jobs)
  • Other services inside your own app
  • Unit and integration tests

If validation lives only in the HTTP layer, every other caller must duplicate that logic — or skip it entirely.

Enter @validate

pico-pydantic solves this by pushing validation into the service method itself. The @validate decorator inspects Pydantic-typed parameters and automatically coerces and validates arguments before the method body runs.

from pico_pydantic import validate
from pydantic import BaseModel

class ItemData(BaseModel):
    name: str
    price: float

class ItemService:
    @validate
    def add_item(self, data: ItemData) -> str:
        return f"Added {data.name} at ${data.price}"

Now add_item({"name": "Widget", "price": 9.99}) works — the plain dict is coerced into an ItemData model. And add_item({"price": -1}) raises a ValidationFailedError with a clear message. The contract is enforced regardless of the caller.

levels.title: Your First @validate
levels.description:
Auto-Coercion with @validate

In services.py, the ItemService has an add_item method that expects an ItemData model. Right now, passing a plain dict will fail because there is no automatic coercion.

Your task: add the @validate decorator to add_item so that a raw dict is automatically converted into an ItemData instance before the method body runs.

files.name: schemas.py
files.content:
from pydantic import BaseModel

class ItemData(BaseModel):
    name: str
    price: float

files.name: services.py
main.py
files.content:
import pico_ioc as pico
import services
import schemas
import pico_pydantic

pico.init(modules=[pico_pydantic, services])

service = pico.get(services.ItemService)

# Passing a plain dict — @validate will coerce it into ItemData
result = service.add_item({"name": "Widget", "price": 9.99})
print(result)

hints: The @validate decorator is already imported from pico_pydantic in services.py.
Place @validate directly above the def add_item line.

The final method should look like:
@validate
def add_item(self, data: ItemData) -> str:

levels.title: Catching Validation Errors
levels.description:
Handling Invalid Data Gracefully

When @validate encounters invalid data, it raises a ValidationFailedError. In test_main.py, one test passes valid data and another passes invalid data (a negative age).

The invalid-data test tries to catch the error, but it's catching the wrong exception type. Your task: fix the except clause in test_invalid_user to catch ValidationFailedError from pico_pydantic.

files.name: schemas.py
files.content:
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class UserCreate(BaseModel):
    username: str = Field(min_length=1)
    age: int = Field(ge=0)

files.name: services.py
test_main.py
files.content:
import pico_ioc as pico
import services
import schemas
import pico_pydantic

pico.init(modules=[pico_pydantic, services])
svc = pico.get(services.UserService)

def test_valid_user():
    result = svc.create_user({"username": "alice", "age": 30})
    assert "alice" in result
    print(f"PASS: {result}")

def test_invalid_user():
    try:
        # Negative age should fail validation
        svc.create_user({"username": "bob", "age": -5})
        assert False, "Should have raised an error"
    # 👈 Fix: catch the correct exception type
    except ValueError:
        print("PASS: Validation error caught correctly")

hints: The error raised by @validate is not a plain ValueError. It is ValidationFailedError from the pico_pydantic module.
You need to import ValidationFailedError from pico_pydantic at the top of test_main.py.
Replace except ValueError: with except pico_pydantic.ValidationFailedError: (or import it and use the short name).

solution.test_main.py:
import pico_ioc as pico
import services
import schemas
import pico_pydantic
from pico_pydantic import ValidationFailedError

pico.init(modules=[pico_pydantic, services])
svc = pico.get(services.UserService)

def test_valid_user():
    result = svc.create_user({"username": "alice", "age": 30})
    assert "alice" in result
    print(f"PASS: {result}")

def test_invalid_user():
    try:
        # Negative age should fail validation
        svc.create_user({"username": "bob", "age": -5})
        assert False, "Should have raised an error"
    # 👈 Fix: catch the correct exception type
    except ValidationFailedError:
        print("PASS: Validation error caught correctly")

levels.title: Complex Validation Rules
levels.description:
Field Constraints and Custom Validators

Pydantic gives you powerful tools: Field constraints, field_validator, and model_validator. Combined with @validate, these rules run automatically on every service call.

In schemas.py, the OrderData model is missing some Field constraints. The tests in test_main.py expect that invalid inputs are rejected. Your task: add the correct Field constraints to make all tests pass.

files.name: schemas.py
services.py
test_main.py

hints: Pydantic's Field supports constraints like min_length for strings and gt (greater than) for numbers.

The final fields should look like:
product: str = Field(min_length=1)
quantity: int = Field(gt=0)
price_per_unit: float = Field(gt=0)

levels.title: Stacking @validate with Interceptors
levels.description:
Composing Validation and Cross-Cutting Concerns

@validate is itself an interceptor in the pico-ioc AOP chain. You can stack it with other interceptors like logging, caching, or authorization.

In services.py, the PaymentService already has @validate. A LoggingInterceptor is defined but not yet wired up. Your task: add @intercepted_by(LoggingInterceptor) after @validate so that both run in the chain. Validation fires first, then logging, then the method body.

files.name: schemas.py
files.content:
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class PaymentData(BaseModel):
    amount: float = Field(gt=0)
    currency: str = Field(min_length=3, max_length=3)

files.name: services.py
main.py
files.content:
import pico_ioc as pico
import services
import schemas
import pico_pydantic

pico.init(modules=[pico_pydantic, services])

svc = pico.get(services.PaymentService)

print("--- Valid payment ---")
result = svc.process_payment({"amount": 42.50, "currency": "USD"})
print(f"Result: {result}")

print("\n--- Invalid payment ---")
try:
    svc.process_payment({"amount": -10, "currency": "USD"})
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Caught: {type(e).__name__}: {e}")

hints: The intercepted_by function is already imported in services.py. You just need to use it as a decorator.
Place @intercepted_by(LoggingInterceptor) on the line between @validate and def process_payment.

The final method should have two decorators stacked:
@validate
@intercepted_by(LoggingInterceptor)
def process_payment(self, data: PaymentData) -> str:
